content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 12, 2015

Steve G. Fox
Law Offices of Steve Fox
42156 N. 10th Street West, Suite 103
Lancaster, CA 93534

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-14-199

Dear Mr. Fox:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May you pay legal expenses in connection with a lawsuit filed by your former legal secretary with campaign funds or set up a legal defense fund to pay for these costs?

CONCLUSION

The expenses incurred to defend against unfair labor practices or wage and hour laws connected to your law practice are solely personal in nature and are not related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. Therefore, you may not use campaign funds or set up a legal defense fund to pay these costs.

FACTS

You are a former Assemblymember. You are being sued by your former business secretary, Kristina Zahn. In her lawsuit, Ms. Zahn alleges that you did not pay her for all the hours she worked at your law office and that you required her to work between 15 to 25 hours for free on your campaign while you ran for a state Assembly seat. She also alleged that she was

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-14-199
Page No. 2


denied full lunch breaks and was not reimbursed for her mileage while running errands for your
law practice.

After you were elected to the California Assembly, you hired Ms. Zahn as a
Secretary/Field Scheduler. She alleged in her lawsuit that in addition to her duties as a state
employee, you also required her to perform work for your law office without compensation.
Ms. Zahn alleges that she maintained your private law office calendar, answered calls from your
clients, drafted trial briefs and other pleadings, and ran errands for your law practice including
driving to court to file documents. During this time she alleges she was not compensated for any
of the work she performed for your law firm.

You deny these allegations and wish to know whether you may use campaign funds to
pay for your defense as some of the allegations arise from your campaign activities. You also
wish to know if you may open a legal defense fund to pay for these legal expenses.

ANALYSIS

Campaign Funds and Personal Use Restrictions

In general, the Act's provisions restricting the "personal use" of campaign funds are
designed to prevent candidates, elected officials, and others who control the expenditures of
campaign funds from benefiting privately from their campaign activities. The general rule is that
an expenditure of campaign funds must be reasonably related to a political, legislative or
governmental purpose. However, where an expenditure of campaign funds confers a substantial
personal benefit² on any individual or individuals with authority to approve the expenditure of
campaign funds held by the committee, the expenditure must be directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89512.)

Section 89510 provides that "[a]ll contributions deposited into the campaign account
shall be deemed to be held in trust for expenses associated with the election of the candidate or
for expenses associated with holding office."

Section 89512 states in pertinent part:

"(a) An expenditure to seek office is within the lawful execution of the trust imposed
by Section 89510 if it is reasonably related to a political purpose. An expenditure
associated with holding office is within the lawful execution of the trust imposed by
Section 89510 if it is reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose.
Expenditures which confer a substantial personal benefit shall be directly related to a
political, legislative or governmental purpose."

² Section 89511(b)(3) defines "substantial personal benefit" as an expenditure of campaign funds which
results in a direct personal benefit with a value of more than $200 to a candidate, elected officer, or any individual or
individuals with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a committee.

File No. A-14-199
Page No. 3


        The payment of a candidate's or elected officer's attorney's fees would confer a
substantial personal benefit on a candidate. Therefore, the expenditures must be directly related
to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.

        Section 89514 governs the use of campaign funds for attorney's fees. It states:

        "Expenditures of campaign funds for attorney's fees and other costs in connection with
administrative, civil, or criminal litigation are not directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose except where the litigation is directly related to
activities of a committee that are consistent with its primary objectives or arises directly
out of a committee's activities or out of a candidate's or elected officer's activities,
duties, or status as a candidate or elected officer, including, but not limited to, an action
to enjoin defamation, defense of an action to enjoin defamation, defense of an action
brought for a violation of state or local campaign, disclosure, or election laws, and an
action arising from an election contest or recount."

        Based upon the facts you have provided, you are defending a lawsuit filed by your former
business secretary for alleged violations of the Labor Code including failure to pay overtime
wages, failure to pay minimum wage and reimbursements for work expenses, failure to provide
meal periods and rest periods, failure to pay wages due and unfair business practices.

        These allegations arise exclusively from her employment with your private law office.
The primary thrust of the lawsuit arises from a labor dispute involving your private law practice
and not out of your activities, duties, or status as a candidate or elected official. While you have
asserted that your former employee alleged in her suit that "she was made to work extra hours
for free on [your] campaigns," this charge is incidental to the primary complaint, in which she
alleges violations of the Labor Code arising from her employment with your law firm.

        Accordingly, the action to defend against unfair labor practices or wage and hour laws
connected to your law practice is solely personal and is not related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. Therefore, attorney's fees may not be paid for from your campaign
funds.

Legal Defense Funds:

        Section 85304 states in part:

        "(a) A candidate for elective state office or an elected state officer may establish a
separate account to defray attorney's fees and other related legal costs incurred for the
candidate's or officer's legal defense if the candidate or officer is subject to one or more
civil or criminal proceedings or administrative proceedings arising directly out of the
conduct of an election campaign, the electoral process, or the performance of the
officer's governmental activities and duties. These funds may be used only to defray
those attorney fees and other related legal costs." (Emphasis added.)

File No. A-14-199
Page No. 4


Legal defense funds are different than general campaign funds and are raised, deposited, and reported under rules that differ from corresponding provisions governing campaign funds. However, generally monies raised and spent by a candidate or elected official under Section 85304 are campaign funds, and are subject to the "personal use" limitations on expenditures of campaign funds. (Section 89513.)

You are a named defendant in a civil proceeding arising out of your conduct as a private employer. Because the proceeding is personal in nature and not directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose, you may not open a legal defense fund to pay for these costs.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division

ER:jgl
